chunks.text:
An Efficient Multimodal System for Chest X-Ray
Analysis with Improved Dataset Generalization
1st Neela Lohitha Susmitha Reddy Udumula
Department of CSE
SRM University AP, India
susmithareddy udumula@srmap.edu.in
2nd Turvi Devineni
Department of CSE
SRM University AP, India
turvi devineni@srmap.edu.in
3rd Abhigna Thallapaneni
Department of CSE
SRM University AP, India
abhigna thallapaneni@srmap.edu.in
4th Akbar Bashee Shaik
Department of CSE
SRM University AP, India
akbarbashee shaik@srmap.edu.in
5th Ajaykumar Sanaka
Department of CSE
SRM University AP, India
ajaykuma sanaka@srmap.edu.in
6th Raushan Kumar Singh
Assistant Professor
Department of CSE
SRM University AP, India
raushankumar.s@srmap.edu.in
Abstract—Chest X-ray images have found wide applications
in identifying various thoracic diseases; however, their correct
interpretation requires considerable time and effort. To address
this issue, in this paper, we introduce a lightweight multimodal
approach for improving Chest disease detection by integrating
both visual and textual data. Our model contains two major
components: a CNN to learn relevant features from chest X-rays
and a basic text encoder to handle radiological reports. These
features are then combined using an attention mechanism. We test our model using the MIMIC-CXR dataset and extend
our evaluation to the CheXpert dataset to examine its generalizability. As shown in our experiments, our model performs
well, scoring an average AUC of 0.986 on the MIMIC-CXR
dataset and 0.738 on the CheXpert dataset, demonstrating its
effectiveness and generalizability. Comparing to other methods,
our approach achieves a good balance between performance and
efficiency. Moreover, its lightweight nature ensures applicability
in real-world scenarios with scarce computational resources. Index Terms—Chest X-ray Analysis, Multimodal Learning,
Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), Attention Mechanism,
Cross-Dataset Generalization
I. INTRODUCTION One of the most frequently employed diagnostic imaging procedures in medical practice, chest X-ray radiography,
plays an important role in the detection and management of
respiratory and cardiovascular disorders such as pneumonia,
atelectasis, cardiomegaly, pleural effusion, and pneumothorax [1]. However, interpretation of chest X-ray images requires
significant expertise, and the scarcity of radiologists worldwide
acts as a major constraint in medical practice, especially in
low- and middle-income countries [2]. Computer-aided diagnosis (CAD) systems based on artificial
intelligence (AI) have been recognized as a game-changer
in this domain, with tremendous potential to automate and
standardize image interpretation on a large scale [3]. Traditionally, image features have been employed in CAD systems,
but clinical image interpretation is a multimodal problem
by design.

A radiologist, while interpreting an X-ray image,
also tries to contextualize their observations with respect
to a patient’s clinical history, previous reports, and textual
annotations on the image
[4]. Keeping this in mind, recent
research in this domain has tried to investigate multimodal
frameworks for image and text fusion to improve diagnostic
accuracy. Real-time image acquisition and processing systems
have long played a foundational role in automated visual
inspection and diagnostic workflows [25], and recent advances
in deep learning have extended these capabilities to complex
medical imaging scenarios such as chest X-ray analysis. While promising results have been achieved with the use of
large multimodal models like BioViL [5], CheXBert [6], and
MedCLIP
[7], these models also have high computational
requirements, making them impossible to use in resourceconstrained environments. Moreover, most existing models
have only been tested within a single dataset, raising questions
of their generalizability. The limitations of the existing approaches are addressed in
the proposed paper, where a computationally efficient multimodal framework is proposed to model visual radiographic
features and clinical text information for multi-label CXR
classification tasks. The main contributions of the proposed
paper can be summarized as follows: First, a lightweight multimodal framework is proposed, where an efficient convolutional
neural network is used to model visual features, along with a
lightweight text encoder, which is fused using a cross-modal
attention fusion framework to model text features. Second, a
rigorous evaluation protocol is proposed, where a high mean
AUC of 0.986 is obtained using five-fold cross-validation on
the MIMIC-CXR dataset, demonstrating strong performance
on the target domain. Third, strong zero-shot cross-dataset
generalization performance is demonstrated, where the proposed model is evaluated on the CheXpert dataset without

fine-tuning, achieving a high mean AUC of 0.738. Fourth, a
detailed per-label evaluation is performed, where nine different
pathological conditions are considered, covering the entire
range of Chest X-Ray diagnostic conditions. II. RELATED WORK
A. Unimodal Deep Learning for Chest X-Ray Analysis
The application of convolutional neural networks for radiographic image classification has been extensively studied
since the seminal work of Wang et al. [8], who introduced the
ChestX-ray14 benchmark and demonstrated that DenseNetbased architectures could achieve clinically meaningful AUC
scores across 14 pathology classes. Subsequently, CheXNet
[9] extended this approach using a 121-layer DenseNet trained
in 112,000 chest radiographs, surpassing the execution of
the radiologist-level in pneumonia detection. More recently,
variants of the EfficientNet [ 10] architecture have been used
for the task of CXR classification, demonstrating state-of-theart used with a significantly smaller number of parameters. However image-based approaches are limited by their inability to capture the semantic content embedded within the
related clinical documentation. Radiological reports provide
contextual information related to the patient’s history, clinical
presentation, and diagnostic decision-making that cannot be
inferred from the radiograph alone [11]. B. Clinical Natural Language Processing
Significant progress has been achieved in the development
of clinical NLP since the introduction of transformer-based
language models [12]. BioBERT [13], a variant of BERT
pre-trained on PubMed abstracts and clinical notes, showed
promising results for text classification and named entity
recognition tasks. ClinicalBERT
[14] is another variant of
BERT, which is pre-trained on hospital admission notes and
clinical records. Recently, CheXBert [6] was proposed, which
utilizes a variant of BERT pre-trained on radiological reports
for extracting structured labels from free-text, comparable to
human annotators. Beyond the clinical domain, the effectiveness of NLP-based
contextual word embeddings for robust text classification has
been demonstrated across diverse real-world domains [24],
further validating the adoption of transformer-based text encoders for structured classification tasks such as radiology
report understanding. C. Multimodal Learning in Medical Imaging
The combination of both visual and textual modalities for
medical image analysis has obtained considerable popularity. ConVIRT [15], proposed by Zhang et al., used contrastive
learning between CXR image pairs and their respective associated radiology reports, thereby enhancing image representation learning. BioViL further enhanced this approach
with the incorporation of phrase grounding objectives for the
alignment of local image regions with phrases in the report. GLoRIA [16] proposed a global-local attention mechanism for
multimodal contrastive learning.

MedCLIP proposed a dataefficient approach using semantic matching instead of relying
on exact pairing. Although these models have achieved considerable success, they often demand large-scale pre-training on millions
of image-report pairs along with considerable computational
requirements. Our work distinguishes itself with its emphasis
on efficiency and generalizability across-datasets. III. METHODOLOGY
A. System Overview
The proposed multimodal framework, illustrated conceptually in Fig. 1, comprises three principal modules: (i)
a lightweight CNN-based image encoder, (ii) a compact
transformer-based text encoder, and (iii) a cross-modal attention fusion module followed by a multi-label classification
head. The design philosophy prioritises parameter efficiency
and inference speed while maintaining competitive diagnostic
accuracy. B. Algorithm 1: Multimodal Chest X-ray Classification
1: Step 1: Data Acquisition
2: Collect chest X-ray images from dataset
3: Obtain associated clinical text
4: Step 2: Data Preprocessing
5: Resize
images
to
standard
resolution
(224×224
or
1024×1024)
6: Normalize image pixel values
7: Tokenize clinical text input
8: Handle missing clinical data using placeholders
9: Step 3: Feature Extraction
10: Extract image features using CNN-based encoder
11: Encode clinical text using transformer-based encoder
12: Step 4: Multi-View Aggregation
13: Combine features from multiple X-ray views
14: Generate unified image representation
15: Step 5: Cross-Modal Fusion
16: Apply attention mechanism to fuse image and text features
17: Generate fused multimodal representation
18: Step 6: Domain Adaptation
19: Apply Gradient Reversal Layer (GRL)
20: Train domain classifier for domain-invariant features
21: Step 7: Classification
22: Predict disease labels using sigmoid activation
23: Perform multi-label classification
24: Step 8: Model Training
25: Compute classification loss (BCE / Asymmetric Loss)
26: Compute domain adversarial loss
27: Optimize model using AdamW optimizer
28: Step 9: Model Evaluation
29: Evaluate using metrics (Accuracy, AUC, mAP)
30: Step 10: Output
31: Return predicted disease classes

Fig. 1. Proposed multimodal framework architecture. C. Image Encoder
The image encoding module adopts a modified EfficientNetB0 [10] backbone pre-trained on ImageNet [17]. EfficientNetB0 was selected for its favourable accuracy-to-parameter ratio,
offering competitive feature extraction capability with substantially fewer floating-point operations compared to VGG [18],
ResNet-50 [19], or DenseNet-121. The input CXR images
are resized to 224 × 224 pixels and normalised using the
ImageNet mean (μ = [0.485, 0.456, 0.406]) and standard
deviation (σ = [0.229, 0.224, 0.225]). Standard data augmentation techniques are applied during
training, including random horizontal flipping (p = 0.5), random rotation (range: ±10◦), and colour jitter (brightness and
contrast perturbation ±0.2). The final convolutional feature
map of dimension 7 × 7 × 1280 is flattened and projected via
a learned linear layer to a d-dimensional image embedding
vector v ∈Rd, where d = 256. D. Text Encoder
Clinical text associated with each CXR study is processed using a lightweight variant of Bio-ClinicalBERT [20],
a knowledge-distilled transformer that retains 97% of the
BERT’s language understanding capability with 40% fewer
parameters and 60% faster inference. Tokenized input text uses
the WordPiece tokenizer with a maximum sequence length
of 128 tokens, based on the average length of radiology
impression sections in the MIMIC-CXR dataset. The [CLS] token representation from the final transformer
layer serves as the global text embedding. This embedding is
projected via a linear layer to same d-dimensional space as
the image embedding, yielding a text embedding t ∈Rd.
E. Cross-Modal Attention Fusion
Rather than simple concatenation, we propose a crossmodal attention fusion module inspired by the cross-attention
mechanism of the Transformer architecture [12]. Given image embedding v and text embedding t, the fusion module
computes attention-weighted representations as follows:
v′ = Attention(Q = v, K = t, V = t)
= softmax
(vWQ)(tWK)⊤
√
d

(tWV )
(1)
t′ = Attention(Q = t, K = v, V = v)
= softmax
(tWQ)(vWK)⊤
√
d

(vWV )
(2)
where WQ, WK, and WV are learnable projection matrices. The attended image representation v′ , text representation t′
are concatenated and passed through a layer normalization
step and a two-layer feed-forward network to produce the final
fused representation f ∈R2d.

F. Multi-Label Classification Head
The fused representation f is fed into a multi-label classification head consisting of a dropout layer (p = 0.3), a
fully connected layer projecting to C output logits (C = 9
pathology classes), and a sigmoid activation function.

The model is trained using weighted binary cross-entropy
loss: L = −
C
X
i=1
wi [yi log(ˆyi) + (1 −yi) log(1 −ˆyi)]
where yi ∈{0, 1} is the ground truth label, ˆyi is the predicted
probability, and wi denotes the weight of the class. G. Training Protocol
The model is optimized using the AdamW optimizer [21]
with an initial learning rate of 3 × 10−4 and weight decay
of 1 × 10−2. A cosine annealing learning rate schedule with
warm restarts is used. Training continues for a maximum of 10
epochs with early stopping based on validation AUC (patience
= 3 epochs). The batch size is set to 32. All experiments
are conducted using PyTorch 2.0 in a CPU-based environment
with 8 threads, demonstrating the practical accessibility of the
framework. IV. EXPERIMENTAL ANALYSIS
A. Datasets
The Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care Chest
X-Ray (MIMIC-CXR) dataset
[22], developed by the MIT
Laboratory for Computational Physiology, contains 227,835
radiographic studies from 64,588 patients admitted to the
Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center. Each study includes
at least one frontal CXR image and a corresponding freetext radiology report. The structured labels for 14 pathological
conditions are provided by CheXpert labeller. For this study, 9
diagnostic labels common to both MIMIC-CXR and CheXpert
are utilized: Atelectasis, Cardiomegaly, Consolidation, Edema,
No Finding, Pneumonia, Pneumothorax, Lung Opacity, and
Fracture. CheXpert
The CheXpert data set
[23] , released by the Stanford
Machine Learning Group, contains 224,316 chest radiographs
from 65,240 patients with structured labels for 14 radiological
observations. The CheXpert validation set (200 studies with
expert-annotated labels) is used exclusively as a zero-shot
cross-dataset test set to evaluate the generalizability of the
model trained solely on MIMIC-CXR. B. Evaluation Protocol
Model performance is assessed using the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve Area (AUC-ROC), consistent with
standard practice in multi-label CXR classification [8]. Fivefold cross-validation is used on MIMIC-CXR. The mean AUC
in all pathology classes is reported as the primary aggregate
metric. Statistical significance for the generalization results
of the cross data set verified using bootstrap confidence
intervals(n = 1000 bootstrap samples). TABLE I

CheXpert (cross-dataset) across 10 epochs
C. Baseline Comparisons
The proposed multimodal framework is benchmarked
against three baseline configurations: (1) a unimodal imageonly EfficientNet-B0 model, (2) a unimodal text-only DistilBERT classifier, and (3) a na ̈ıve feature-concatenation multimodal model without the cross-modal attention fusion mechanism. This ablation structure isolates the contribution of each
component. V. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION
A. In-Domain Performance on MIMIC-CXR
Table I reports per-label AUC scores achieved by the
proposed framework on the MIMIC-CXR in-domain validation set. The model achieves a mean AUC of 0.986, with
individual class AUC scores ranging from 0.984 (Edema)
to 0.997 (Cardiomegaly), demonstrating consistently strong
performance across all diagnostic categories. The training dynamics are visualized in Fig. 2, which shows
the evolution of training loss and validation AUC over 10
training epochs. The model converges rapidly, achieving a
validation AUC above 0.90 within the first few epochs and
stabilizing around 0.986 by epoch 8. The steady decrease in
training loss alongside the consistent increase in validation
AUC indicates stable optimization and effective learning without signs of overfitting. B. Cross-Dataset Generalization on CheXpert
Table II presents cross-dataset performance in the CheXpert
validation set without any target-domain fine-tuning. The
proposed framework achieves a mean AUC of 0.738 in nine

pathological classes. In particular, classes with well-defined
visual and textual signatures, such as Atelectasis (1.000),
Edema (1.000) and No Finding (1.000), generalize perfectly. In
contrast, classes characterized by high inter-observer variability and annotation inconsistencies between datasets, namely
Consolidation (0.440), Pneumothorax (0.446) and Fracture
(0.205), exhibit reduced cross-dataset performance. Fig. 3.
Per-label AUC scores for nine pathologies on MIMIC-CXR and
CheXpert datasets. This performance pattern is consistent with known challenges in cross-dataset CXR evaluation. Irvin et al. [23]
documented significant differences in the labeling conventions
for Consolidation and Pneumonia between CheXpert and other
CXR benchmarks, attributed to divergent radiology report generation practices across institutions. The differential labeling of
uncertain findings using uncertain mapping strategies further
complicates cross-dataset alignment
C. Comparison with Baseline Models
Table III compares the proposed multimodal framework
against baseline configurations on the MIMIC-CXR validation set. The image-only EfficientNet-B0 baseline achieves a
mean AUC of 0.961, while the text-only DistilBERT baseline
achieves 0.942. Na ̈ıve concatenation fusion improves over unimodal baselines (mean AUC: 0.974), but the proposed crossmodal attention fusion achieves the highest overall performance (mean AUC: 0.986), confirming that structured modality interaction is superior to simple feature concatenation. D. Comparison with State-of-the-Art
Table IV contextualizes the performance of the proposed
framework against representative state-of-the-art multimodal

methods on CXR classification benchmarks. Although largescale pre-trained models such as BioViL and MedCLIP
achieve marginally higher AUC scores on in-domain evaluation, these methods require orders of magnitude more parameters (200M–400M vs. 12M for the proposed framework) and
extensive pre-training on millions of image-report pairs.

The
proposed framework provides competitive performance with
much lower computational overhead; therefore, it is a feasible
option for practical deployment in environments where access
to large-scale GPU environments is not available. E. Training Epoch Analysis
Table V shows the training loss, validation AUC, and crossvalidation AUC for the CheXpert dataset over the course of
training epochs. This gives us a better idea of the training
dynamics. We can observe a constant decrease in the training
loss from 0.4734 at epoch 1 to 0.0208 at epoch 10, while the
validation AUC is increased monotonically from 0.8714 to
0.9868. The cross-validation AUC peaks at 0.8705 at epoch 1
and stays between 0.73 and 0.78 for the rest of the epochs,
indicating a certain degree of domain adaptation. F. Error Analysis and Limitations
The lower performance on the reduced cross-dataset for
Consolidation, Pneumothorax, and Fracture requires further
analysis. Analysis of misclassified cases reveals two primary
failure modes: (i) label ambiguity arising from differences in
annotation conventions between MIMIC-CXR and CheXpert
particularly regarding the treatment of uncertain findings and
(ii) domain shift in image acquisition parameters, including
differing exposure settings, patient positioning conventions,
and scanner characteristics across institutions. A notable limitation of the current framework is its reliance on the impression section of radiology reports. In real-world clinical
settings, the availability and completeness of structured text
annotations cannot be guaranteed at inference time. Future
work will explore text-free inference pathways and uncertainty
quantification to enable graceful degradation when textual
input is absent or unreliable.

VI. CONCLUSION
This paper has presented a lightweight, efficient multimodal
framework for automated multi-label chest X-Ray diagnosis
that jointly models visual radiographic features and clinical text information. The proposed architecture integrates
an EfficientNet-B0 image encoder, a Bio-ClinicalBERT text
encoder, and a cross-modal attention fusion mechanism within
a compact 12M-parameter model. Rigorous evaluation on
the MIMIC-CXR dataset through five-fold cross-validation
demonstrates a mean AUC of 0.986, and zero-shot crossdataset evaluation on CheXpert achieves a mean AUC of
0.738, confirming meaningful generalizability without any
target-domain fine-tuning. Ablation studies confirm the additive contributions of each modality and the superiority
of cross-modal attention fusion over na ̈ıve concatenation. Compared to existing large-scale multimodal methods, the
proposed framework achieves competitive diagnostic accuracy
with substantially lower computational requirements, making
it a practical candidate for deployment in resource-constrained
clinical environments. Future work will explore (i) domain
adaptation techniques to improve cross-dataset performance
for ambiguously labeled pathology classes, (ii) uncertaintyaware inference for clinical decision support, (iii) extension to
include structured clinical metadata (age, sex, comorbidities)
as a third modality, and (iv) prospective clinical validation in
collaboration with radiologists. REFERENCES
